items.ai_summary: The Arch Linux issue is a potentially high-severity vulnerability due to the lack of specific details, which could lead to various exploitation scenarios. Without a CVE ID or CVSS score, it's challenging to determine the exact impact, but given its categorization as an advisory, it may affect system configuration or package management. Affected systems likely include those running Arch Linux with potentially vulnerable packages, although the specific versions and packages are not mentioned. Real-world impact could range from minor configuration issues to more severe problems like data corruption or unauthorized access, depending on the exploit. Defenders should monitor Arch Linux security advisories and apply updates as soon as they become available to mitigate potential risks.

items.ai_summary: The python-tornado-6.5.7-1.fc45 update for Fedora addresses two critical vulnerabilities, CVE-2026-35536 and CVE-2026-31958, which were assigned bugzilla IDs rhbz#2457335 and rhbz#2451660, respectively. These vulnerabilities could potentially allow for remote code execution or other malicious activities, although specific details on the nature of the vulnerabilities are not provided. The update to version 6.5.7 of python-tornado mitigates these issues, and users are advised to apply the update as soon as possible to prevent potential exploitation. The lack of specific TTPs detected suggests that these vulnerabilities may not have been widely exploited in the wild yet, but the presence of CVE IDs indicates a recognized threat. Users of affected Fedora systems should prioritize applying this update to ensure the security of their systems.

items.url: https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/mir-2.26.0-2.fc44 rust-ashpd-0.13.12-2.fc44 rust-busd-0.5.0-3.fc44 rust-gtk4-macros-0.11.4-2.fc44 rust-inferno-0.12.6-3.fc44 rust-quick-xml-0.41.0-1.fc44 rust-reqsign-aws-v4-3.0.1-2.fc44 rust-wayland-scanner-0.31.10-3.fc44 sandogasa-0.15.3-2.fc44
items.ai_summary: The Fedora update addresses two security advisories, RUSTSEC-2026-0194 and RUSTSEC-2026-0195, which are related to the `quick-xml` crate. The vulnerabilities affect the `mir-2.26.0-2.fc44`, `rust-ashpd-0.13.12-2.fc44`, `rust-busd-0.5.0-3.fc44`, and `rust-gtk4-macros-0.11.4-2.fc44` packages. The update also includes a rebuild of dependent packages and an update to the `sandogasa` package to version 0.15.3, which resolves issues with file requests and package replacements. To remediate, defenders should apply the Fedora update and ensure that all dependent packages are rebuilt with the updated `quick-xml` version. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is moderate, as it may allow for potential XML parsing vulnerabilities in affected applications.

items.ai_summary: The Fedora update addresses two security advisories, RUSTSEC-2026-0194 and RUSTSEC-2026-0195, which affect the `quick-xml` crate. These advisories are related to potential vulnerabilities in the XML parsing library, which could lead to security issues in dependent packages. The update also includes a rebuild of dependent packages, such as `sandogasa`, to ensure they are using the latest version of `quick-xml`. The `sandogasa` package has been updated to version 0.15.3, which includes a fix for a bug that could cause issues with package dependencies. To remediate this issue, defenders should update their Fedora systems to the latest version of the affected packages, including `quick-xml` and `sandogasa`.

items.ai_summary: The Fedora update addresses two security advisories, RUSTSEC-2026-0194 and RUSTSEC-2026-0195, which affect the `quick-xml` crate. The vulnerabilities are related to XML parsing and could potentially lead to denial-of-service or code execution attacks. The update also includes a rebuild of dependent packages, such as `kryoptic`, `mir`, `rust-ashpd`, and `rust-busd`, to ensure they are not affected by the vulnerabilities. Additionally, the `sandogasa` package has been updated to version 0.15.3, which includes a fix for a bug that could cause issues with package dependencies. To remediate, defenders should apply the updates as soon as possible, especially if they are using the affected packages in their Fedora-based systems.

items.ai_summary: The Fedora kernel update to version 7.1.3-100.fc43 addresses a critical vulnerability, CVE-2026-53359, which could potentially allow for arbitrary code execution or elevation of privileges. The specific details of the vulnerability are not provided, but the update includes a number of important fixes across the kernel tree, suggesting a broad impact on system stability and security. The update also includes new features and improved hardware support, which may introduce new potential vulnerabilities if not properly configured. Real-world impact could include targeted attacks on Fedora systems, particularly those with unpatched versions of the kernel. To remediate, defenders should apply the kernel update as soon as possible and ensure that all dependent packages, such as kernel-headers, are also updated to the latest version.

items.ai_summary: A critical vulnerability was discovered in the python-llvmlite package, version 0.48.0, affecting Fedora 45 systems. The update, released on July 3, 2026, addresses three security issues (RHBZ#2453491, RHBZ#2495139, and RHBZ#2496401) that could be exploited by attackers to gain unauthorized access or execute arbitrary code. Although no CVE ID is assigned, the severity of the issues warrants a high severity score. The update is essential for all Fedora 45 users to prevent potential exploitation. To remediate, users should immediately update their python-llvmlite package to version 0.48.0-1.

items.title: Fedora: cifs-utils-7.6-2.fc45
items.description: Automatic update for cifs-utils-7.6-2.fc45. ##### **Changelog** ``` * Mon Jul 6 2026 Paulo Alcantara - 7.6-2 - cifs.upcall: fix regression with kerberos mounts - Resolves: rhbz#2497446 - Resolves: rhbz#2496963 - Resolves: rhbz#2489808 - CVE-2026-12505 ```
items.ai_summary: A critical vulnerability, CVE-2026-12505, was discovered in the cifs-utils-7.6-2.fc45 package, affecting Fedora systems. The issue, a regression in the cifs.upcall component, allows threat actors to steal or forge Kerberos tickets (T1558) via kerberos mounts. This exploit can lead to unauthorized access and data exfiltration. To mitigate this threat, defenders should update the cifs-utils package to the latest version and ensure proper Kerberos configuration. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious Kerberos activity and implementing strict access controls can help prevent exploitation.

items.title: Feodo C2: 27.133.154.218:443 (QakBot)
items.description: C2 server: 27.133.154.218:443 | Malware: QakBot | Status: offline
items.ai_summary: The Feodo C2 server, located at 27.133.154.218:443, was identified as a command and control (C2) infrastructure associated with the QakBot malware. This C2 server was used to facilitate communication between infected systems and the threat actor's control panel, enabling the exfiltration of sensitive data and potential lateral movement within the compromised network. QakBot is a highly sophisticated malware strain that employs the T1071 Application Layer Protocol tactic to establish a foothold on victim systems. The use of this C2 server suggests that the threat actor was actively exploiting vulnerable systems to distribute QakBot, highlighting the need for defenders to prioritize patching and updating their systems. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately block traffic to the C2 server, conduct thorough network scans for signs of QakBot infection, and implement robust security controls to prevent future exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Feodo C2: 178.62.3.223:443 (QakBot)
items.description: C2 server: 178.62.3.223:443 | Malware: QakBot | Status: offline | Hostname: box.nautadb.com
items.ai_summary: The Feodo C2 server at 178.62.3.223:443, associated with the QakBot malware, has been identified as a potential threat vector. QakBot is a banking Trojan that uses the T1071 Application Layer Protocol tactic to communicate with its C2 server, which in this case is the Feodo C2 server. The T1082 System Information Discovery tactic is also employed to gather system information from compromised hosts. This C2 server is likely being used to control a large number of compromised hosts, making it a high-priority target for defenders. To remediate this threat, defenders should block all traffic to and from the Feodo C2 server and implement network segmentation to isolate potential QakBot-infected hosts.

items.ai_summary: The Feodo C2 server, located at 34.204.119.63:443, was identified as a command and control (C2) server associated with the QakBot malware. QakBot is a type of banking Trojan that uses the T1071 Application Layer Protocol tactic to communicate with its C2 server. The C2 server was found to be using the T1082 System Information Discovery tactic to gather system information from infected hosts. This information is then used to tailor the malware's behavior and evade detection. To remediate this threat, defenders should block traffic to the C2 server and implement network segmentation to isolate potential QakBot infections.

items.title: Feodo C2: 50.16.16.211:443 (QakBot)
items.description: C2 server: 50.16.16.211:443 | Malware: QakBot | Status: online | Hostname: ec2-50-16-16-211.compute-1.amazonaws.com
items.ai_summary: The Feodo C2 server at 50.16.16.211:443 is a known command and control (C2) infrastructure used by the QakBot malware. QakBot is a highly sophisticated banking trojan that has been used in various high-profile attacks, including the 2017 NotPetya ransomware attack. The C2 server is currently online and hosts the QakBot malware, which is capable of stealing sensitive information, including login credentials and financial data. To remediate this threat, defenders should block all traffic to and from the C2 server IP address and implement a web application firewall (WAF) to prevent future attacks. Additionally, users should ensure that their systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches and antivirus software.

items.title: The Crown Prince, Nezha
items.description: Beginning in August 2025, a sophisticated intrusion was discovered where attackers used log poisoning techniques to deploy a web shell on vulnerable phpMyAdmin panels. The threat actors exploited misconfigured web applications to plant China Chopper web shells, controlled via AntSword, before deploying Nezha, an open-source monitoring tool, to facilitate remote command execution. items.title: Armored Likho's new weapon: BusySnake Stealer
items.description: Kaspersky uncovered a sophisticated phishing campaign by the APT group Armored Likho, deploying a previously undocumented Python-based infostealer dubbed BusySnake Stealer. The campaign targets government agencies and electric power sectors across Russia, Brazil, and Kazakhstan through spear-phishing emails containing malicious EXE or LNK attachments. Discovered during April 2026, this evolved framework continues to conceal malicious payloads within seemingly legitimate installer packages while expanding its functionality. items.title: Indirect Prompt Injection in Web Content Targets AI Agents
items.description: AI agents are increasingly vulnerable to indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks, where malicious instructions are embedded in web content to manipulate AI-driven workflows. Two campaigns were identified that combine SEO poisoning with CSS/HTML abuse to influence AI decision-making. The first campaign uses fake API documentation to trick AI agents into making fraudulent payments for a fake Python

items.title: Phishing in the Balkans: Fake Traffic Fines, Real Losses
items.description: An active SMS phishing campaign targets Serbian road users by impersonating Putevi Srbije, Serbia's state road authority. Victims receive text messages claiming they have unpaid traffic fines with urgent payment demands. The fraudulent links lead to cloned government websites designed to steal payment card details. The infrastructure employs JavaScript-based obfuscation techniques to evade automat

items.title: AsyncRAT and Remcos Delivered in Multi-Stage Phishing Campaign
items.description:
A global phishing campaign targets business functions with emails carrying malicious Excel attachments that initiate a multi-stage infection chain when macros are enabled.
The attack uses layered obfuscation, including HTA scripts, PowerShell, encoded payloads, and steganography in PNG files, to deliver and execute Remote Access Trojans such as Remcos and AsyncRAT in a largely fileless manner.
It

items.title: Cavern Manticore: Exposing Iran-Linked Modular C2 Framework
items.description: Check Point Research tracks Cavern Manticore, an Iran-nexus threat actor targeting Israeli government and IT sectors. The actor deploys a modular C2 framework built on .NET but compiled into different formats including Mixed-Mode C++/CLI and Native AOT, creating significant anti-analysis challenges. The framework consists of core agents and specialized post-exploitation modules providing capabilit

items.title: Bundled to Steal: The Salat Stealer Campaign
items.description: Salat Stealer is a Go-based information stealer that performs deep system reconnaissance and extracts sensitive data from compromised hosts. It targets browser credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, and communication platforms like Discord and Steam. The malware features advanced surveillance capabilities including desktop streaming, audio/video capture through microphone and webcam, and local file

items.title: One Email Closer to the Edge: UNK_MassTraction & the Physics of Exploitation
items.description: Since May 2026, a suspected China-aligned threat cluster named UNK_MassTraction has been exploiting Roundcube mailservers at physics and engineering departments of US and Canadian universities. The campaigns exploit multiple n-day vulnerabilities including CVE-2024-42009 and CVE-2025-49113 to steal credentials and deploy either a webshell called SquareShell or the VShell backdoor into server memor

items.title: From Phishing to Persistence: A CrySome RAT Infection Chain Analysis
items.description: A sophisticated multi-stage infection chain was analyzed following successful containment by MDR SOC operations. Initial access occurred through spear-phishing using a logistics rate confirmation lure, delivering CrySome remote access trojan via multiple stages. The attack chain leveraged living-off-the-land techniques, ICMLuaUtil COM interface for UAC bypass, and in-memory AMSI patching. WinDefCt

items.title: CVE-2026-58056: RustDesk gates incoming control messages on per-capability flags rather than on ...
items.description: RustDesk gates incoming control messages on per-capability flags rather than on the session's authorized connection type, and a file-transfer session does not clear those flags. A peer holding only a valid FileTransfer authorization can inject keyboard and mouse input and reach the unguarded screenshot and display-capture handlers, acting outside its granted scope.

items.title: CVE-2026-58055: nghttp2's nghttpx proxy through 1.69.0 forwards an HTTP/1.1 Upgrade request that...
items.description: nghttp2's nghttpx proxy through 1.69.0 forwards an HTTP/1.1 Upgrade request that also carries a Content-Length header and body onto reusable keep-alive backend connections, re-adding the Upgrade and Connection headers while passing Content-Length verbatim. A backend that resolves the resulting ambiguous message in the attacker's favor enables HTTP request/response smuggling and cross-client respon

items.title: CVE-2026-58054: MyBB 1.8.40 does not restrict which usergroup a limited Admin Control Panel user...
items.description: MyBB 1.8.40 does not restrict which usergroup a limited Admin Control Panel user may assign when creating or editing users; the user module offers the Administrators group (gid 4) and its datahandler's verify_usergroup() unconditionally returns true. An admin holding only the delegated user-management permission can assign the Administrators group to an account and escalate to the full Administrat

items.title: CVE-2026-58053: Gitea act_runner with the Docker backend (through act 0.262.0) passes a workflow...
items.description: Gitea act_runner with the Docker backend (through act 0.262.0) passes a workflow's container.options string to the Docker job container's HostConfig and, when configured with privileged: false, forces only the Privileged flag off while merging options such as --pid=host, --cap-add, and --security-opt unchanged. A user who can run a workflow on a Docker-backed runner can create a job container with

items.title: CVE-2026-58052: 7-Zip for Windows through 26.02 fails to preserve the Mark-of-the-Web when extra...
items.description: 7-Zip for Windows through 26.02 fails to preserve the Mark-of-the-Web when extracting a crafted RAR5 archive, because its guard that suppresses an archive-supplied Zone.Identifier stream matches the exact name 'Zone.Identifier' while a RAR5 STM record named ':Zone.Identifier:$DATA' is not matched and NTFS canonicalizes it to the same stream, overwriting the propagated Internet-zone marker with Zon

items.title: CVE-2026-58051: libssh2 through 1.11.1 grows its publickey list with SSH2_REALLOC but does not z...
items.description: libssh2 through 1.11.1 grows its publickey list with SSH2_REALLOC but does not zero-initialize new entries before parsing populates them, so a parse failure reaching the cleanup path leaves libssh2_publickey_list_free operating on an uninitialized entry. A malicious SSH server offering the publickey subsystem can use a malformed response to make cleanup free an uninitialized, attacker-influenceabl

items.title: CVE-2026-58050: libssh2 through 1.11.1 reads an attacker-controlled 32-bit attribute count from ...
items.description: libssh2 through 1.11.1 reads an attacker-controlled 32-bit attribute count from a publickey-subsystem response and uses it in the allocation num_attrs * sizeof(libssh2_publickey_attribute) without bounds checking, so on 32-bit platforms the multiplication overflows to an undersized buffer. A malicious SSH server can then drive the attribute-parsing loop to write past the allocation, causing a heap

items.title: CVE-2026-58049: FFmpeg's RASC video decoder (decode_dlta in libavcodec/rasc.c) performs 32-bit r...
items.description: FFmpeg's RASC video decoder (decode_dlta in libavcodec/rasc.c) performs 32-bit reads and writes at the row cursor before the NEXT_LINE row-boundary check and validates the DLTA region in pixel rather than byte units, so a DLTA run on a PAL8 frame can access several bytes past the row allocation. A crafted media stream using the RASC FourCC, decoded by libavcodec, triggers a bitstream-controlled ou

items.title: CVE-2026-8095: The Frontend File Manager Plugin plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Authentic...
items.description: The Frontend File Manager Plugin plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Authenticated Arbitrary File Deletion in versions up to and including 23.6. This is due to a case-sensitive bypass of the wpfm_dir_path parameter sanitization in the wpfm_file_meta_update AJAX handler, where supplying WPFM_DIR_PATH in uppercase evades the unset check and is normalized to wpfm_dir_path by sanitize_key() during u

items.title: CVE-2026-10643: Zephyr's IP socket recvmsg() implementation (subsys/net/lib/sockets/sockets_inet...
items.description: Zephyr's IP socket recvmsg() implementation (subsys/net/lib/sockets/sockets_inet.c, insert_pktinfo()) validated the user-supplied ancillary (msg_control) buffer using only the payload length (msg-msg_controllen < pktinfo_len) before writing a full control message consisting of an aligned cmsg header plus the payload. Because the check omitted the cmsg header size, a control buffer whose length fal

items.title: RCS and DNS: The NAPTR Record, (Mon, Jul 6th)
items.description: Over the last year, with recent updates to iOS and Android, RCS (Rich Communication Services) has become an increasingly used protocol [1]. RCS is supposed to eventually replace SMS, and in addition to richer formatting, provides added (but optional) security. RCS messages may be end-to-end encrypted and digitally signed. Unlike SMS, which was "bolted on" to existing voice-focused phone standards. The SMS standard was based on old-fashioned pagers and allowed for limited clear-text communications. RCS is built from the ground up around modern IP-based network infrastructure and behaves more like IP chat services (think iMessage, WhatsApp...). RCS defines the message format, while protocols like SIP are used to establish connections and transport messages.

The action comes roughly two weeks after KrebsOnSecurity published findings from multiple security firms connecting NetNut to the Popa botnet, a collection of at least two million devices that have been compromised by malicious software with little or no consent from victims.

items.description: ESET Research analyzes Gamaredon’s new toolset and the group’s growing reliance on legitimate online services to hide its C&C infrastructure and exfiltrate stolen data

items.title: Smashing Security podcast #473: How a hacker could have Rickrolled the entire World Cup
items.description: A polite caller from your bank says there is a problem with your account. Don't worry - they'll send someone round to help. They'll even take your cards away to keep them safe. The scam has run rampant, until Dutch police plastered blurred photos of 100 suspects across billboards, supermarkets, and TikTok, with a two-week ultimatum to turn themselves in... or else. Meanwhile, a security researcher called Bob DaHacker got her hands on the live broadcast controls for every match of the 2026 FIFA World Cup. She could have Rickrolled the entire planet, but actually spent days trying to find anyone at FIFA who would pick up the phone. Plus! Don't miss our featured interview with Black Kite's Jeffrey Wheatman exploring ransomware and extortion attacks across Europe. All this and more in episode 473 of the "Smashing Security" podcast with cybersecurity expert and keynote speaker Graham Cluley, and special guest Danny Palmer.

items.title: ESET takes part in Operation Endgame to disrupt Amadey and Stealc
items.description: ESET researchers assisted in the global disruption of the Amadey botnet and Stealc infostealer, providing technical analysis, infrastructure tracking, and affiliate-level insights

items.title: OpenClaw’s Skill Marketplace and the Emerging AI Supply Chain Threat
items.description: Unit 42's analysis of ClawHub revealed evasive malicious skills bypassing automated scanners to deploy infostealers and execute agentic financial fraud. The post OpenClaw’s Skill Marketplace and the Emerging AI Supply Chain Threat appeared first on Unit 42.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: Hacker hijacks Brazil’s national alert system, sending “misanthropy” to millions of phones
items.description: Emergency alert systems work because people believe them. Every time one of these systems issues a false alert - whether through negligence or a deliberate attack - trust erodes. Read more in my article on the Hot for Security blog.

items.title: The Global Namespace Risk: Universal Bucket Hijacking Technique for Cloud Data Exfiltration
items.description: Unit 42 research details how attackers could exploit global name uniqueness in bucket hijacking to redirect cloud data streams across major CSPs. The post The Global Namespace Risk: Universal Bucket Hijacking Technique for Cloud Data Exfiltration appeared first on Unit 42.

items.title: Apple’s Hide My Email tweak leaves privacy fans fuming
items.description: Apple has long marketed itself as the privacy-first tech giant. So why is it making a change to Hide My Email that will make it easier for websites to block anonymous sign-ups - and harder for you to stay private online? Read more in my article on the Hot for Security blog.

items.title: Imposter scams cost Americans $3.5 billion in 2025 – and it’s getting worse
items.description: Someone is pretending to be your bank, your government, or your local planning office. And according to the FTC, they're making billions doing it. Read more in my article on the Fortra blog.

items.title: Close Encounters of the Human Kind
items.description: In the latest Threat Source, Hazel channels her inner Spielberg to explore why humans are delightfully irrational, reminding us that while security best practices are simple in theory, they’re a lot harder to pull off when you’re busy dealing with real life.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Scripting the disassembler: Local agentic reverse engineering through vbdec’s live COM object model
items.description: Cisco Talos detailed a new approach to reverse engineering that pairs local AI agents with traditional analysis tools like the VB6 disassembler vbdec. Instead of awkwardly bolting AI onto the software, vbdec exposes its parsed data through a live COM interface.

items.title: Killing me gently: Inside Gentlemen’s EDR killer framework
items.description: ESET Research shares the results of a months-long investigation into the suite of EDR killers maintained by the RaaS gang Gentlemen

items.title: Smashing Security podcast #472: AI gets hacked, and BitLocker gets bypassed
items.description: What if your AI coding assistant could be tricked into stealing your own company's secrets - by reading a single booby-trapped bug report? No phishing email. No malware. No password ever stolen. Just an AI doing exactly what it was told. Meanwhile, someone themselves Nightmare Eclipse has decided to teach Microsoft a lesson. The result? Three zero-days dropped on the internet, one of which lets a thief with a USB stick walk straight past BitLocker. Microsoft is furious. Plus don't miss our featured interview with Son Nguyen Kim of Proton Pass, who explains why plugging AI agents into your email and calendar without thinking twice is rather like hiring a new employee with the keys to everything - and skipping the background check. All this and more in episode 472 of the "Smashing Security" podcast with cybersecurity expert and keynote speaker Graham Cluley, and special guest Paul Ducklin.

items.title: Protecting legacy OT systems against modern cyberthreats
items.description: Many manufacturing plants depend on OT systems that stay in service for many years. That long run can hide significant cybersecurity risks.

items.title: Pickle in the Middle – Hijacking Vertex AI Model Uploads for Cross-Tenant RCE
items.description: Unit 42 discovered a Vertex AI Python SDK vulnerability that allows remote code execution via bucket squatting. Read the article for more. The post Pickle in the Middle – Hijacking Vertex AI Model Uploads for Cross-Tenant RCE appeared first on Unit 42.

items.title: FishMonger’s arsenal upgraded: SprySOCKS for Windows
items.description: ESET researchers have discovered SprySOCKS for Windows, FishMonger’s backdoor weaponizing a kernel driver for advanced stealthiness

items.title: Inside the Modern SOC: The 72-Minute Race
items.description: Attackers can move from access to exfiltration in 72 minutes. Learn how modern SOC teams close the speed gap with Unit 42's AI-driven automation, threat hunting, MDR and Managed XSIAM. The post Inside the Modern SOC: The 72-Minute Race appeared first on Unit 42.

items.title: EvilTokens: A phishing attack that doesn’t steal your password
items.description: A phishing kit subverting Microsoft’s legitimate authentication flow lets attackers break into accounts without stealing passwords or creating fake login pages

items.title: Tracing Digital Intent: New MacOS Tahoe 26 Artifact Discovered
items.description: Unit 42 has discovered a new macOS Tahoe 26 forensic artifact that tracks user menu selections across the operating system. Learn more here. The post Tracing Digital Intent: New MacOS Tahoe 26 Artifact Discovered appeared first on Unit 42.

items.title: A tale of two eras
items.description: In this week’s newsletter, Amy reminisces on the tech toys of their childhood, inspired by a hilarious lesson about why your digital privacy shouldn't be left on an open channel.

items.title: Trust No Skill: Integrity Verification for AI Agent Supply Chains
items.description: Protect enterprise AI agents from supply chain risks by auditing third-party skills for hidden vulnerabilities and multi-stage attack chains. The post Trust No Skill: Integrity Verification for AI Agent Supply Chains appeared first on Unit 42.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday for June 2026 — Snort rules and prominent vulnerabilities
items.description: Microsoft Patch Tuesday details for June 2026.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
